text.fullText: From: Julie Bernstein <noreply@adv.actionnetwork.org> To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 1/21/2026 3:11:08 PM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 -------------------------------------------------------- Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. The Environmental Protection Agency, The proposed changes to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act would be extremely detrimental.. For 50 years, this provision has provided essential protection for our nation's waterways by giving states and authorized tribes the ability to review federally permitted projects that could affect their water quality. The proposed rule would significantly limit this authority by restricting reviews to only "direct discharges" into waterways, rather than allowing comprehensive assessments of broader environmental impacts. This change would weaken local communities' ability to protect drinking water sources, fisheries, wetlands, and cultural resources. While a fuel pipeline or an LNG port may not involve any direct discharge, they still pose a real threat if there is a leak. We know that these threats have caused huge environmental impacts when they occur so these eventualities must be part of any siting decision. tEven a manufacturer that intends to abide to limitations on the discharge of toxic pollutants can pose a serious danger to a community if for example, they are not abiding by stringent regulations to mitigate against a fire risk. It should absolutely be the business of a nearby community to ensure that before such a company is allowed to operate that they have laid out how they will mi tigate against any fire hazard and that they have in place to alert everyone in the surrounding community immediately if a fire breaks out and will then be responsible for monitoring the impact of any contamination to local waterways and for any necessary cleanup or even supply of drinking water should a reservoir or the water table impact local well water. Furthermore, the proposed strict deadlines and limited review scope would accelerate project approvals without ensuring adequate environmental protection. This comes at a particularly concerning time, following recent judicial decisions that have already reduced federal water protections. Water is a shared resource that sustains communities, ecosystems, and economies. Once contaminated, restoration can take generations. The current Section 401 authority provides essential safeguards that allow local communities to protect their water resources for current and future generations. I urge the EPA to withdraw this proposed rule and maintain the current authority that allows states and tribes to conduct meaningful water quality reviews. Our nation's waterways deserve comprehensive protection, not weakened standards that prioritize expedited project approvals over environmental stewardship. Sincerely, Julie Bernstein Julie Massachusetts
